title: Cry, the Beloved Country
author: Alan Paton
synopsis: The story of Reverend Stephen Kumalo, a Zulu priest who travels from his rural village to Johannesburg to find his sister and son, only to discover the harsh realities of apartheid South Africa.
context: Written during apartheid, the novel is a plea for reconciliation and understanding between South Africa's divided communities.

characters.name: Stephen Kumalo
characters.description: A dedicated parish priest from Ndotsheni who journeys to Johannesburg seeking his family.

characters.name: Absalom Kumalo
characters.description: Stephen's son who moves to Johannesburg seeking work and becomes entangled in crime.

characters.name: Gertrude Kumalo
characters.description: Stephen's sister who moves to Johannesburg and becomes a prostitute.

characters.name: James Jarvis
characters.description: A white South African farmer whose murdered son's writings inspire him to help the black community.

characters.name: Arthur Jarvis
characters.description: A white South African activist working for reform and racial justice, murdered by Absalom.

characters.name: Mapiere Kumalo
characters.description: Stephen's wife who remains in Ndotsheni tending to their home.

themes.name: Apartheid and Segregation
themes.description: The destructive effects of racial division and discrimination.
examples: Johannesburg's contrast to the poverty of Ndotsheni
The racial tension throughout the novel
The separate and unequal treatment of black South Africans

themes.name: Reconciliation
themes.description: The possibility of healing through understanding and forgiveness.
examples: James Jarvis's forgiveness of Absalom despite his son's death
Kumalo's dignified speech at the trial
The meeting between Kumalo and James Jarvis

themes.name: Fear
themes.description: How fear drives both black and white communities.
examples: Fear in Ndotsheni about the failing crops
The white farmers' fear of the black population

themes.name: Nature and the Land
themes.description: The relationship between people and the land they live on.
examples: The contrast between the red soil of Ndotsheni and Johannesburg
The symbol of the broken dam representing societal breakdown

quotes.text: Cry, the beloved country, for the unborn child that is drawn to the yonder passed.
quotes.speaker: Poem/Song
quotes.context: The opening poem that frames the novel and establishes its themes

quotes.text: I have one great fear in my heart, that one day when they are turned to loving, they will find that we are turned to hating.
quotes.speaker: Stephen Kumalo
quotes.context: Expressing his fear about the consequences of oppression

quotes.text: There is no love in the world like this love, this love that has no price and no demand.
quotes.speaker: Stephen Kumalo
quotes.context: About the unconditional love of a parent for a child

quotes.text: The tragedy is not that things are broken. The tragedy is that things are not mended again.
quotes.speaker: Narrator
quotes.context: Reflecting on the broken state of South African society

quotes.text: It was the man who walked the road in the darkness that came upon the great rock of stumbling.
quotes.speaker: Narrator
quotes.context: Describing Kumalo's journey and the obstacles he faces

quotes.text: It suited the white man to break the tribe, but it has not suited him to build something in the place of what is broken.
quotes.speaker: James Jarvis
